description: The provided image visually displays a software user-interface element labeled "Check for updates" and does not contain any discernible scientific figure content, micrographs, spectra, or data plots relevant to materials science. Based solely on the accompanying text_extraction_summary_json for the paper on Fe–20Mn–6Al–0.6C–0.15Si austenitic low-density steel, the study investigates temperature-dependent tensile deformation and Charpy impact toughness at -196°C, -77°C, and room temperature, presenting engineering stress-strain curves, work-hardening rate analyses, EBSD orientation maps, TEM micrographs of deformation twins and microbands, SEM fracture surface morphologies, and instrumented Charpy load-deflection curves. The extracted text reports yield strengths increasing from 400 MPa at room temperature to 900 MPa at -196°C, with elongation rising from 32% to 68%, attributed to planar glide, microband formation, and mechanical twinning at lower temperatures. However, these details are derived from the provided structured text summary rather than direct visual observation of the image.

Figure 1 presents the initial microstructure and phase constitution of the as-forged Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steel. Panel (a) is an EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) orientation map (scale bar 50 μm) displaying equiaxed grains with colors referenced to the standard IPF triangle (001–101–111). Panel (b) is the corresponding EBSD phase map, where the matrix appears uniformly red with black grain-boundary lines, indicating a single-phase field. Panel (c) shows X-ray diffraction patterns of the initial steel (black curve) and after holding at −196 °C for 30 min (red curve); both patterns contain only austenite peaks labeled γ(111), γ(200), γ(220), and γ(311), with no evidence of martensite reflections. The caption states that the initial microstructure is fully austenitic and that no isothermal martensite forms during cryogenic exposure. Collectively, the EBSD maps and XRD patterns demonstrate that the austenite phase is exceptionally stable down to −196 °C.

Figure 2 presents the tensile test results of Fe-20Mn-6Al-0.6C austenitic low-density steel at -196°C, -77°C, and room temperature (RT). Panel (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves where both strength and elongation increase markedly as temperature decreases. Panel (b) plots true stress-strain curves (solid lines) alongside work hardening rate curves (dashed lines); the work hardening rate drops sharply at the elastic-to-plastic transition, then remains near-linear at cryogenic temperatures while decreasing continuously at RT, with the necking point defined where true stress equals the work hardening rate. Panel (c) summarizes the negative temperature dependence of tensile properties: total elongation (TE) increases from approximately 32% at RT to 68% at -196°C, and ultimate tensile strength (UTS) rises from about 800 MPa to 1300 MPa, following the linear relations UTS=813.0-2.36*T and YS=474.6-2.05*T. This anomalous cryogenic strengthening and ductility enhancement is attributed to decreasing stacking fault energy promoting dislocation planar glide and mechanical twinning.

This figure presents comprehensive EBSD analyses of the tensile-deformed Fe–20Mn–6Al–0.6C austenitic low-density steel at three deformation temperatures. Panels (a–c) are inverse pole figure (IPF) maps showing crystal directions parallel to the tensile direction, where the deformed austenite grains concentrate in {111} and {001} orientations; white arrows in (a) mark mechanical twins formed at −196 °C. Panels (d–f) are grain boundary (GB) maps where red lines represent low-angle grain boundaries (LAGBs, 2–15°) and black lines represent high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs, >15°). The LAGB fraction increases systematically as temperature decreases: 56.0 % at room temperature, 63.0 % at −77 °C, and 70.2 % at −196 °C, while the HAGB fraction decreases correspondingly. Panels (g–i) are geometrically necessary dislocation (GND) distribution maps (kernel average misorientation scale 0–3°) superposed with GB maps for the regions framed in (d–f), showing that GNDs preferentially accumulate at LAGBs. These results indicate that lowering the deformation temperature promotes planar glide, increases LAGB density, and intensifies local dislocation accumulation, consistent with reduced stacking fault energy.

This figure presents TEM analysis results comparing the deformation microstructures of a Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steel after tensile deformation at cryogenic temperatures. Subfigure (a) shows a bright-field TEM image of the sample deformed at −196 °C, where mechanical twins and dislocations are visible within the austenite matrix; the inset provides the corresponding SAED pattern indexed with a [1,1,0] zone axis, confirming the twinning orientation relationship. Subfigure (b) displays the microstructure of the sample deformed at −77 °C, revealing dislocations and traces of {111} slip planes associated with uniformly distributed microbands, with no mechanical twins observed. These images demonstrate a temperature-dependent transition in deformation mechanisms: as the deformation temperature decreases and the stacking fault energy drops, the dominant mechanism shifts from planar glide producing microbands at −77 °C to mechanical twinning on {111} planes along <112> directions at −196 °C. Both microbands and mechanical twins act as barriers to dislocation motion, thereby contributing to a high work hardening rate.

Figure 5 presents instrumented Charpy impact test results for Fe-20Mn-6Al-0.6C-0.15Si austenitic low-density steel at -196°C, -77°C and room temperature. Panel (a) shows load-deflection curves where the maximum load increases with decreasing temperature (approximately 32 kN at -196°C, 27 kN at -77°C and 24 kN at RT), while the deflection at fracture decreases markedly, indicating a ductile-to-brittle transition under impact. Panel (b) decomposes total absorbed energy into initiation (W_initiation), stable crack propagation (W_stable) and unstable propagation (W_unstable) components. Total energy decreases from 327 J at RT to 277 J at -77°C and 188 J at -196°C, driven primarily by a drastic reduction in W_stable at cryogenic temperatures, whereas W_initiation stays relatively high. These results demonstrate that the steel retains high strength but suffers reduced cryogenic impact toughness because stable crack propagation energy diminishes significantly.

The figure presents fracture surface morphologies of Charpy V-notch impact samples of Fe–20Mn–6Al–0.6C–0.15Si austenitic low-density steel tested at −196 °C (a, d), −77 °C (b, e), and room temperature (c, f). Panels (a)–(c) show low-magnification fracture surface views with inset 3D topographic images, revealing the V-notch, radial zone, shear lip, and fibrous zone—the latter being nearly absent at −196 °C but progressively enlarged at −77 °C and RT. Panels (d)–(f) provide corresponding high-magnification SEM images of the fracture microstructure. At −196 °C (d), the surface exhibits large dimples and tear edges indicating a quasi-cleavage tendency with limited plastic deformation. At −77 °C (e) and RT (f), increasingly well-developed dimple patterns signify more ductile fracture behavior. The overall trend demonstrates a transition from predominantly ductile tearing at room temperature toward a flatter, less fibrous, more brittle-appearing fracture at cryogenic temperatures, consistent with reduced impact energy absorption at lower temperatures. Shear lips are visible across all tested temperatures.

Fig. 7 is a schematic illustration depicting the generalized temperature dependence of tensile elongation in austenitic steels as a function of tensile test temperature or stacking fault energy (SFE). The horizontal axis represents increasing tensile test temperature/SFE from left to right, while the vertical axis shows tensile elongation. The curve exhibits a peak that divides the response into three characteristic regimes. Region I (high temperature/SFE, right) is labeled "Wavy glide" and corresponds to lower elongation. Region II (intermediate temperature/SFE, center) is labeled "Planar glide except DIMT" and shows the maximum tensile elongation at the peak. Region III (low temperature/SFE, left) is labeled "DIMT" (deformation-induced martensitic transformation), where elongation decreases with decreasing SFE. An arrow at the top indicates that glide planarity is enhanced toward the left (lower temperature/SFE). The figure conveys that an optimal intermediate SFE range (Region II) maximizes ductility through planar glide mechanisms such as mechanical twinning and microbands, which is relevant to the investigated Fe–20Mn–6Al–0.6C–0.15Si austenitic low-density steel.

This figure presents a Hollomon-type logarithmic plot of true stress versus true strain (lnσ versus lnε) for determining the strain-hardening exponents of a Fe–20Mn–6Al–0.6C–0.15Si austenitic low-density steel under tensile loading at room temperature (gray squares), −77 °C (red circles), and −196 °C (blue triangles). Each curve is fitted with two linear segments representing the low-strain (n₁) and high-strain (n₂) work-hardening stages. The extracted exponents show a monotonic increase with decreasing temperature: n₁ rises from 0.174 at room temperature to 0.181 at −77 °C and 0.204 at −196 °C, while n₂ increases from 0.362 to 0.439 and 0.448, respectively. The pronounced elevation of both n₁ and n₂ at cryogenic temperatures indicates significantly enhanced work-hardening capacity, which correlates with the temperature-dependent transition from wavy dislocation glide at room temperature to planar glide and mechanical twinning at cryogenic conditions. The dual-stage behavior reflects an evolving dominance of deformation mechanisms with accumulated strain.

Figure 9 presents instrumented Charpy impact load-deflection curves and temperature-dependent characteristic values for a Fe-20Mn-6Al-0.6C-0.15Si austenitic low-density steel. Subfigures (a)-(c) show smoothed experimental and ISO-evaluation curves at -196°C, -77°C, and room temperature, respectively, identifying general yield load (F_gy), crack initiation load (F_in), maximum load (F_m), unstable crack growth initiation load (F_iu), and final load (F_a). The shaded areas partition absorbed impact energy into crack initiation (W_initiation), stable crack growth (W_stable), unstable crack growth (W_unstable), and collapse (W_collapse) stages, with rebound compliance (RC) values of 10.9957, 4.6215, and 3.7864, respectively, indicating faster unstable crack propagation at lower temperatures. Subfigure (d) plots F_gy, F_m, and F_in versus temperature (left axis, kN) alongside the F_iu/F_m ratio (right axis), demonstrating that these loads and the ratio increase as temperature decreases. The figure supports the conclusion that the steel exhibits higher crack initiation energy at cryogenic temperatures—W_initiation increases from approximately 57 J at room temperature to 112 J at -196°C—but reduced stable and unstable crack propagation energy, compromising overall cryogenic impact toughness despite enhanced resistance to crack initiation.

Figure 10 presents EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) maps and a local misorientation profile for the cross-section near the fracture surface of an Fe–20Mn–6Al–0.6C–0.15Si austenitic low-density steel after Charpy impact testing. Subfigure (a) shows the microstructure at −196 °C, while (b) shows the microstructure at room temperature (RT), both using a 200 μm scale bar and the standard [001]–[101]–[111] orientation triangle. At both temperatures, the primary crack paths exhibit a zigzag morphology characteristic of transgranular fracture. In (b), Grain A is explicitly marked near the lower-right crack, and secondary cracks are visible at RT. Subfigure (c) plots the point-to-point misorientation along the marked line traversing Grain A; the misorientation difference between the regions on either side of the crack is less than 15°, confirming that both regions originally belonged to the same austenite grain. This provides direct evidence for transgranular fracture. The curve also reveals significant local orientation gradients adjacent to the crack, indicating severe plastic deformation during crack propagation. The comparison supports that secondary cracks at RT relieve stress concentration at primary crack tips and delay fracture, whereas their absence at −196 °C correlates with lower crack propagation energy.

Figure 11 consists of scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs showing the regions immediately beneath the fracture surfaces of Fe–20Mn–6Al–0.6C–0.15Si austenitic low-density steel after Charpy impact testing at (a) −196 °C, (b) −77 °C, and (c) room temperature (RT). Yellow arrows indicate planar glide traces—aligned slip bands or deformation markings—in the near-fracture microstructure. At −196 °C, planar glide is scarce and restricted to isolated regions, whereas at −77 °C these features become more numerous and pronounced. At RT, planar glide is most extensive and densely distributed. This temperature dependence demonstrates that although reduced stacking fault energy favors glide planarity during low-strain-rate tensile deformation, the higher activation energy required for slip at cryogenic temperatures inhibits planar glide activation under high-strain-rate impact conditions. As a result, the paucity of planar glide barriers at −196 °C offers less resistance to crack propagation, explaining the experimentally observed poor cryogenic impact toughness despite the steel’s increased tensile ductility at lower temperatures. All images share a 20 μm scale bar.
